title: Neonatal Care (Leave and Pay) Act 2023
chapters: 6 sections (enabling Act), supplemented by the Neonatal Care Leave and Miscellaneous Amendments Regulations 2025 (SI 2025/375) and the Statutory Neonatal Care Pay (General) Regulations 2025

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Neonatal Care (Leave and Pay) Bill [Private Members' Bill]
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Stuart C. McDonald MP (SNP, Cumbernauld, Kilsyth and Kirkintilloch East) — Private Members' Bill via ballot procedure, top of ballot
commonsSponsors: Stuart C. McDonald MP (SNP — original sponsor)
otherParliamentaryChampions: David Linden MP (SNP, Glasgow East — chair APPG on Premature and Sick Babies, personal experience)
Luke Hall MP (Conservative, Thornbury and Yate — personal experience with premature baby)
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: The Act is an enabling Act — the detailed provisions are in the Regulations. Neonatal Care Leave and Miscellaneous Amendments Regulations 2025 (SI 2025/375) and Statutory Neonatal Care Pay (General) Regulations 2025, both in force 6 April 2025. Applies only to children born on or after 6 April 2025. The Conservative 2019 manifesto committed to introducing neonatal care leave and pay.

secondReadingCommons.summary: Stuart McDonald introduced the Bill stating that parents would be entitled to up to 12 weeks of protected neonatal care leave — one week for each week their baby spends in neonatal care. Luke Hall MP (Conservative) spoke powerfully about his family's personal experience with a premature baby. David Linden MP (SNP, APPG chair) described the Bill as a 'light at the end of the tunnel' in a difficult parliamentary week. Cross-party support was unanimous.

secondReadingLords.summary: Baroness Wyld sponsored the Bill in the Lords. Lord Patel, a neonatologist, spoke about the direct health benefits of parental presence for neonatal babies. The Minister confirmed the Government was targeting April 2025 for commencement and that the legislation was designed to mirror existing parental leave structures for effective integration with HMRC and employer payroll systems.

regulationsDebate.summary: Minister confirmed around 40,000 babies spend over one week in neonatal care each year, an estimated 60,000 parents will be eligible, and approximately 34,000 parents will take up paid neonatal care leave annually. Baroness Wyld praised the cross-party working and noted this was 'one of the best examples of cross-party working' she had seen. Small employers recover 103% of statutory payments from the Exchequer; larger employers recover 92%.

significantCommitteeAmendments.amendment: No substantive amendments — Bill passed Committee without change
significantCommitteeAmendments.summary: The Bill enjoyed total cross-party consensus and passed through both Houses without substantive amendment. The only significant point of debate was the scope of 'neonatal care' — whether it should be limited to neonatal units or extend to home-based care with consultant oversight. The Regulations ultimately adopted a broader definition including ongoing consultant-supervised care post-discharge.
significantCommitteeAmendments.outcome: Enacted as introduced — scope defined broadly in regulations

precedingDocuments.title: Conservative Party Manifesto 2019
precedingDocuments.significance: The 2019 manifesto explicitly committed to introducing neonatal care leave and pay, stating the Government would 'legislate to allow parents to take extended leave for neonatal care, to support those new mothers and fathers who need it during the most vulnerable period of their lives.'

precedingDocuments.title: Petitions Committee: The impact of Covid-19 on maternity and parental leave (HC 526, 2019-21)
precedingDocuments.significance: The Committee's investigation into pandemic impacts on parental leave highlighted the acute vulnerability of parents with babies in neonatal care during lockdown restrictions. Directly cited in the Second Reading debate.

precedingDocuments.title: e-Petition 606011: Introduce neonatal leave and pay entitlement immediately
precedingDocuments.significance: Public petition calling for immediate introduction of neonatal leave, demonstrating significant public support for the legislation.

precedingDocuments.title: Bliss charity — campaign for neonatal care leave
precedingDocuments.significance: Bliss, the leading neonatal charity, ran a sustained campaign for neonatal care leave. Their CEO Caroline Lee-Davey stated that the most supportive employers had relied on 'sick leave and compassionate leave or other improvised solutions, creating challenges for HR teams to navigate.'

precedingDocuments.title: David Linden MP ten-minute rule Bill (2018)
precedingDocuments.significance: David Linden MP (SNP) introduced a ten-minute rule Bill on neonatal leave in 2018, five years before the 2023 Act received Royal Assent. This earlier initiative built parliamentary awareness and was directly credited by Linden at Second Reading.

leadingCases.name: No reported tribunal decisions under the Neonatal Care Act 2023 as at March 2026
leadingCases.citation: N/A — Act in force from 6 April 2025
leadingCases.principle: The Act has been in force for less than one year as at March 2026, and applies only to children born on or after 6 April 2025. No tribunal decisions have been reported. Key areas for future judicial development: scope of 'neonatal care' (hospital vs home-based consultant care), interaction with maternity leave rights, and detriment/automatic unfair dismissal claims.
leadingCases.significance: SURVEILLANCE — Eileen must monitor for first decisions. The complexity of the two-tier leave structure and the interaction with other statutory leave types are likely to generate disputes.

sections.title: Neonatal care leave entitlement — who qualifies and when
sections.summary: Creates a new statutory entitlement to neonatal care leave for employees with parental responsibility for a baby receiving neonatal care. The qualifying conditions are: (1) the baby must be admitted to neonatal care within 28 days of birth (the day after birth counts as day one), (2) the baby must receive at least 7 consecutive days of neonatal care, and (3) the employee must have a qualifying parental relationship with the child (birth parent, intended parent under surrogacy, adopter, or partner of any of these). Neonatal care leave is a Day 1 right — no qualifying period of service. The right applies only to children born on or after 6 April 2025.
sections.text:
The Secretary of State must make regulations entitling an employee to be absent from work on leave in order to care for a child who is receiving neonatal care.

Key qualifying conditions (Neonatal Care Leave and Miscellaneous Amendments Regulations 2025):

(1) The child must begin receiving neonatal care within 28 days of birth.

(2) The child must receive at least 7 consecutive days of neonatal care without interruption.

(3) 'Neonatal care' means:
(a) medical care received by a child in a hospital,
(b) medical care received by a child at any other place, following a period of care in hospital, under the ongoing supervision of a consultant.

(4) The employee must have a qualifying relationship with the child:
(a) be the mother, father, or partner of the mother/father,
(b) be the intended parent in a surrogacy arrangement,
(c) be a prospective adopter who has been matched or placed, or
(d) be the partner of any of the above.

(5) No qualifying period of employment is required — Day 1 right.

(6) Applies only to children born on or after 6 April 2025.
sections.keyPrinciple: Three conditions must be met: neonatal care starting within 28 days of birth, lasting at least 7 consecutive days, and a qualifying parental relationship. 'Neonatal care' is broader than just neonatal intensive care units — it includes consultant-supervised care post-discharge. The Day 1 right means even employees in their first week of employment are eligible.
refs: Neonatal Care Leave and Miscellaneous Amendments Regulations 2025 (SI 2025/375)
Statutory Neonatal Care Pay (General) Regulations 2025
ERA 1996 Part 8 Chapter 5 (new chapter inserted by the Act)
ACAS guidance on neonatal care leave and pay

regulatoryGuidance.title: Neonatal care leave and pay — what the law says
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Comprehensive ACAS guidance with worked examples for tier 1/tier 2 leave, notice requirements, and interaction with other statutory leave

regulatoryGuidance.title: Employer guide to statutory neonatal care pay and leave
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Detailed employer technical guide covering eligibility, records, pay calculation, and HMRC recovery

commonErrors: Requiring a qualifying period of service for neonatal care LEAVE — leave is a Day 1 right with no service requirement (note: statutory neonatal care PAY does require 26 weeks' service)
Limiting 'neonatal care' to care in a neonatal intensive care unit (NICU) — the definition extends to any medical care in hospital AND consultant-supervised care post-discharge
Applying the right to children born before 6 April 2025 — the Act applies ONLY to children born on or after 6 April 2025
Granting additional leave for multiple births — if twins or triplets are in neonatal care simultaneously, the maximum entitlement remains 12 weeks per employee
Confusing 'neonatal care' with any period in hospital — the 7 consecutive days must be continuous neonatal CARE, not just time in hospital for other reasons

Assuming the Act only covers premature babies — it covers ANY baby who receives neonatal care within 28 days of birth and for at least 7 consecutive days. A full-term baby born at 40 weeks who develops a serious infection requiring 10 days in hospital qualifies equally

Limiting 'neonatal care' to neonatal intensive care units (NICU) — the definition extends to all levels of neonatal hospital care including Special Care Baby Units (SCBU), transitional care wards, and Local Neonatal Units (LNU). It also extends to consultant-supervised medical care at home or other settings following discharge from hospital

Withdrawing accrued leave entitlement if the baby dies during neonatal care — the Regulations (Schedule, as modified) preserve the leave already accrued at the point of the child's death. The parent retains the right to take any weeks of neonatal care leave that had already accrued before the death. This is an extremely sensitive area requiring careful and compassionate handling

parts.title: Part 2 — Leave Entitlement: The Two-Tier Structure

sections.title: Amount and timing of neonatal care leave — Tier 1 and Tier 2
sections.summary: Employees are entitled to one week of leave for each week the baby receives neonatal care, up to a maximum of 12 weeks. Leave cannot begin until the baby has been in neonatal care for at least 7 consecutive days. The leave operates under a two-tier structure: Tier 1 covers the period while the baby is receiving neonatal care and one week after care ends (leave taken in weekly blocks, not necessarily consecutive). Tier 2 covers the period after Tier 1 until 68 weeks from the date of birth (leave must be taken in a single continuous block). Leave is ADDITIONAL to all other statutory leave entitlements.
sections.text:
Amount of leave:

(1) One week of neonatal care leave for each complete week the baby receives neonatal care.

(2) Minimum: 1 week. Maximum: 12 weeks.

(3) Leave cannot begin until the second week of neonatal care (after the first 7 consecutive qualifying days).

Two-tier structure:

Tier 1 — During neonatal care + 1 week after:
(a) Covers the period while the baby is receiving neonatal care and one week after the care ends.
(b) Leave can be taken in weekly blocks — need not be consecutive.
(c) The employee can take some weeks during care and save remaining weeks for later (within Tier 2).
(d) Notice: before the employee is due to start work on the first day of absence in that week. No requirement for written notice during Tier 1.

Tier 2 — After care ends, before 68 weeks from birth:
(a) Covers the period after Tier 1 ends and before the end of 68 weeks from the date of the child's birth.
(b) Leave MUST be taken in one continuous block.
(c) Notice: 15 days for one week's leave; 28 days for two or more weeks' leave. Written notice required.
(d) Typically used by parents tagging neonatal care leave onto the end of maternity, paternity, or adoption leave.

All neonatal care leave must be taken within 68 weeks of the child's birth (or placement for adoption).
sections.keyPrinciple: The two-tier structure reflects the reality of neonatal care: Tier 1 allows flexible weekly blocks during the acute period when the baby is in hospital; Tier 2 allows a continuous block later, typically tagged onto other parental leave. The 68-week overall window ensures all leave is taken within the first year and a quarter of the child's life.
refs: ERA 1996 Part 8 Chapter 5 (neonatal care leave)
Neonatal Care Leave Regulations 2025 Regs.6-9 (amount and timing of leave)
ERA 1996 s.71-73 (maternity leave — interaction)
ERA 1996 s.80A (paternity leave — interaction)
ERA 1996 s.75A-75D (adoption leave — interaction)
ACAS guidance — worked examples for tier 1 and tier 2 scenarios
commonErrors: Allowing neonatal care leave to start during the first 7 days of neonatal care — leave cannot start until after the 7 consecutive qualifying days are complete. The employee should use time off for dependants (s.57A) or other leave for the first 7 days
Requiring Tier 1 leave to be taken consecutively — Tier 1 can be taken in non-consecutive weekly blocks
Allowing Tier 2 leave to be taken in separate blocks — Tier 2 must be taken in a single continuous block
Missing the 68-week overall window — all neonatal care leave must be taken within 68 weeks of birth. Leave not taken within this window is forfeited
Failing to recognise that neonatal care leave is ADDITIONAL to paternity, maternity, adoption, and shared parental leave — it does not replace or reduce any other entitlement

sections.title: Notice requirements — Tier 1 and Tier 2 differences
sections.summary: Notice requirements differ significantly between the two tiers. Tier 1 (during neonatal care): the employee must give notice before they are due to start work on the first day of absence — no requirement for written notice, and the employer can agree to accept notice from a family member. Tier 2 (after care): 15 days' written notice for one week's leave, or 28 days' written notice for two or more weeks' leave. Both the employer and employee can mutually agree to waive notice requirements entirely.
sections.text:
Tier 1 notice:
(1) The employee must give notice before they are due to start work on the first day of absence in the relevant week.
(2) No requirement for the notice to be in writing.
(3) ACAS guidance recommends employers consider accepting notice from a family member given the hospital setting.

Tier 2 notice:
(1) For one week's leave: at least 15 days' written notice before the first day of leave.
(2) For two or more weeks' leave: at least 28 days' written notice before the first day of leave.

Waiver:
(1) The employer and employee can mutually agree to waive all notice requirements.
(2) The ACAS guidance recommends employers consider waiving notice requirements given the stressful circumstances.
sections.keyPrinciple: Tier 1 notice is deliberately minimal — the parent may be in a hospital with a critically ill baby and should not be expected to provide formal written notice. Tier 2 notice is more structured because it is typically planned in advance. Employers should consider waiving notice requirements entirely as a matter of good practice.
refs: ACAS neonatal care leave guidance (notice requirements section)
Neonatal Care Leave Regulations 2025 Regs.10-12
ERA 1996 s.57A (time off for dependants — alternative for the first 7 days)
commonErrors: Requiring written notice for Tier 1 leave — written notice is not required during the acute neonatal care period
Refusing to accept notice from a family member — ACAS recommends employers consider this given the parent may be in a hospital setting
Applying Tier 2 notice requirements to Tier 1 leave — the notice regimes are different and Tier 1 is deliberately flexible

sections.num: s.2 + Statutory Neonatal Care Pay (General) Regulations 2025
sections.title: Statutory neonatal care pay — eligibility, rate, and employer recovery
sections.summary: Unlike neonatal care leave (Day 1 right), statutory neonatal care pay (SNCP) requires: (1) 26 weeks' continuous service at the end of the 15th week before the expected week of childbirth, and (2) average earnings of at least £125 per week. The pay rate is £187.18 per week (2025-26) or 90% of average weekly earnings, whichever is lower. Pay is for whole weeks only. Employers administer the payment on behalf of the Government and can recover costs: small employers recover 103%, larger employers recover 92%.
sections.text:
Eligibility for statutory neonatal care pay:

(1) The employee must have at least 26 weeks' continuous service at the end of the 15th week before the expected week of childbirth (or equivalent qualifying period for adoption).

(2) The employee must earn, on average, at least £125 per week before tax (2025-26 rate).

(3) The employee must meet all the conditions for neonatal care leave.

Pay rate:
(1) £187.18 per week (2025-26 financial year), or
(2) 90% of the employee's average weekly earnings,
whichever is lower.

(3) Pay is for whole weeks only — no pro-rata for part-weeks.

Maximum: 12 weeks' pay, mirroring the leave entitlement.

Employer recovery:
(1) Small employers (qualifying for Small Employers' Relief): recover 103% from HMRC.
(2) Larger employers: recover 92% from HMRC.
(3) The 8% employer contribution for larger employers mirrors the arrangement for statutory maternity, paternity, and shared parental pay.

Notice for pay:
(1) Tier 1: written notice within 28 days of the first day claimed.
(2) Tier 2: 15 days' written notice for one week; 28 days for two or more weeks.

Evidence: the employee must provide a written declaration including the child's date of birth, relationship to the child, and dates of neonatal care.

Records: the employer must keep records of SNCP payments for 3 years from the end of the relevant tax year.
sections.keyPrinciple: Leave is a Day 1 right; pay requires 26 weeks' service. This means an employee in their first week of employment can take unpaid neonatal care leave but cannot receive SNCP. The 103% recovery for small employers means the Exchequer subsidises the administrative cost — small businesses are made more than whole.
refs: Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992 Part 12ZE (inserted by the Act)
Statutory Neonatal Care Pay (General) Regulations 2025
ERA 1996 s.166-167 (employee's statutory rights to payment — enforcement route)
HMRC employer guide to statutory neonatal care pay recovery
commonErrors: Confusing leave eligibility with pay eligibility — leave is Day 1 right, pay requires 26 weeks' service AND minimum earnings of £125/week
Failing to administer SNCP through payroll — the employer administers the payment, not HMRC directly. The employer claims recovery from HMRC
Not keeping records for 3 years — records of SNCP payments must be retained for 3 years from the end of the relevant tax year
Paying SNCP for part-weeks — pay is for whole weeks only
Not claiming the HMRC recovery — small employers recover 103% (more than the cost), larger employers recover 92%

Paying statutory neonatal care pay during a period when the employee is entitled to statutory sick pay (SSP) — Regulation 12 of the Statutory Neonatal Care Pay (General) Regulations 2025 provides that there is no liability to pay SNCP in respect of any week during any part of which the employee is entitled to SSP. The employee cannot receive both simultaneously

Failing to consider whether the employee can work during neonatal care leave on a 'Keeping in Touch' (KIT) day equivalent basis — the Regulations do not create a formal KIT day mechanism for neonatal care leave (unlike maternity leave which has 10 KIT days and shared parental leave which has 20 SPLIT days). Employers and employees who wish to arrange occasional contact or work during NCL should agree this on a voluntary basis, being mindful that any compulsion to work could constitute detriment

parts.title: Part 4 — Rights During Leave, Return to Work, and Redundancy Protection

sections.title: Rights during neonatal care leave — terms, conditions, return to work, and enhanced redundancy protection
sections.summary: During neonatal care leave, the employee retains all terms and conditions of employment except pay (unless SNCP is payable). Annual leave continues to accrue. On return, the employee is entitled to return to the same job (or, if that is not reasonably practicable following a combination of statutory leave, a suitable alternative). The Regulations also extend enhanced redundancy protection to employees on or returning from neonatal care leave — the same enhanced protection that applies during maternity leave. This means that if redundancy arises during or shortly after neonatal care leave, the employee must be offered a suitable alternative vacancy in preference to other employees.
sections.text:
Rights during leave:
(1) All terms and conditions continue except remuneration (unless SNCP is payable).
(2) Annual leave, pension contributions, and contractual benefits continue to accrue.
(3) The employee remains bound by obligations of good faith, confidentiality, and notice.

Return to work:
(1) After neonatal care leave taken alone: right to return to the SAME job.
(2) After neonatal care leave combined with other statutory leave totalling more than 26 weeks: right to return to the same job or, if not reasonably practicable, a suitable alternative job on no less favourable terms.

Enhanced redundancy protection:
(1) During neonatal care leave and for a protected period after return: the employee must be offered a suitable alternative vacancy if one exists, in preference to other employees.
(2) This mirrors the enhanced redundancy protection for employees on maternity, adoption, and shared parental leave (as extended by the Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023).
sections.keyPrinciple: Neonatal care leave carries the same employment protections as maternity leave — including enhanced redundancy protection. An employer making redundancies during or shortly after neonatal care leave must offer the affected employee any suitable alternative vacancy, even if other employees are equally or more suitable for the role.
refs: ERA 1996 s.71-73 (maternity leave rights — parallel protections)
Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 (enhanced redundancy protection — now extended to neonatal care leave)
Maternity and Parental Leave etc Regulations 1999 Reg.20 (redundancy during maternity leave — source model)
ERA 1996 s.47C (detriment protection — see Part 5)
commonErrors: Failing to offer a suitable alternative vacancy to an employee on neonatal care leave when making redundancies — enhanced redundancy protection applies

Assuming the employee must return to a different job after combined statutory leave — the right to return to the SAME job applies unless the total statutory leave exceeds 26 weeks AND it is not reasonably practicable
Not accruing annual leave during unpaid neonatal care leave — holiday entitlement continues to accrue regardless of whether SNCP is payable

parts.title: Part 5 — Enforcement: Detriment and Automatic Unfair Dismissal

sections.title: Protection from detriment and automatic unfair dismissal for neonatal care leave
sections.summary: Two layers of protection mirror the maternity leave protection model. First, ERA 1996 s.47C (as amended) protects employees from detriment on the ground that they took or sought to take neonatal care leave, or that the employer believed the employee was likely to take neonatal care leave. Second, ERA 1996 s.99 (as amended) makes it automatically unfair to dismiss an employee for a reason connected with neonatal care leave. No qualifying period of service is required for automatic unfair dismissal claims. These protections apply to both the birth parent and the partner/other parent — both are equally protected.
sections.text:
Detriment protection (ERA 1996 s.47C as amended):
(1) An employee has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by the employer on the ground that the employee took, sought to take, or made use of the benefits of neonatal care leave.
(2) Protection extends to the employer's belief that the employee was likely to take neonatal care leave.

Automatic unfair dismissal (ERA 1996 s.99 as amended):
(1) Dismissal is automatically unfair if the reason or principal reason is connected with neonatal care leave.
(2) No qualifying period of employment is required (ERA s.108(3) exclusion).
(3) Protection applies to both parents — the birth parent and the partner/other qualifying person.

Scope of detriment:
(a) Denial of promotion, training, or development opportunities.
(b) Reduction in hours, changes to shifts, or exclusion from rotas.
(c) Negative performance reviews influenced by the period of absence.
(d) Failure to offer a suitable alternative vacancy during redundancy.
(e) Any other detrimental treatment connected to the leave.
sections.keyPrinciple: Both parents are fully protected. Dismissing either parent — the birth mother OR the partner — for taking or requesting neonatal care leave is automatically unfair dismissal with no qualifying period and potentially uncapped compensation. The 'belief' ground prevents employers from taking pre-emptive action against employees whose babies are known to be in neonatal care.
refs: ERA 1996 s.47C (detriment — neonatal care leave now added to protected grounds)
ERA 1996 s.99 (automatic unfair dismissal — neonatal care leave now added)
ERA 1996 s.108(3) (qualifying period exclusions — neonatal care leave now listed)
ERA 1996 s.104 (assertion of statutory right — additional protection route)
Equality Act 2010 s.18 (pregnancy and maternity discrimination — potential overlap for birth mothers)
commonErrors: Dismissing or disciplining a partner/father for taking neonatal care leave — both parents are equally protected, not just the birth mother
Subjecting an employee to negative performance consequences based on their neonatal care leave absence — the leave period must be disregarded in performance assessments
Assuming two years' service is required to claim unfair dismissal for neonatal care leave reasons — no qualifying period applies for automatic unfair dismissal on this ground

Failing to recognise the 'belief' ground — treating an employee differently because the employer knows their baby is in neonatal care, even before the employee has requested leave, is unlawful detriment

parts.title: Part 6 — Interaction with Other Statutory Leave and Practical Considerations

sections.num: Cross-reference: interaction with statutory leave framework
sections.title: Neonatal care leave in the context of the full statutory leave framework
sections.summary: Neonatal care leave is ADDITIONAL to all existing statutory leave entitlements. It does not replace or reduce maternity leave (52 weeks), paternity leave (2 weeks), adoption leave (52 weeks), shared parental leave, parental leave (18 weeks per child), or carer's leave (1 week per year). Neonatal care leave can be 'tagged on' to the end of other statutory leave. Employees cannot take two types of statutory leave simultaneously — they sequence. The typical pattern is: maternity/paternity leave first, then neonatal care leave tagged on at the end. For the first 7 days before neonatal care leave can begin, the employee should use time off for dependants (s.57A) if not already on other statutory leave.
sections.text:
Interaction map:

(1) Maternity leave (ERA 1996 s.71-73): SEPARATE entitlement. A mother on maternity leave whose baby is in neonatal care accrues neonatal care leave entitlement while on maternity leave, and can tag NCL onto the end of maternity leave.

(2) Paternity leave (ERA 1996 s.80A): SEPARATE entitlement. A father/partner can take paternity leave AND neonatal care leave. NCL is typically tagged onto the end of paternity leave.

(3) Shared parental leave (ERA 1996 s.75E-75G): SEPARATE entitlement. NCL is additional.

(4) Adoption leave (ERA 1996 s.75A-75D): SEPARATE entitlement. NCL applies to adopters whose child requires neonatal care.

(5) Time off for dependants (ERA 1996 s.57A): Used for the FIRST 7 days of neonatal care before NCL can begin, if the employee is not already on other statutory leave.

(6) Carer's leave (Carer's Leave Act 2023): SEPARATE entitlement. Carer's leave covers long-term care needs for dependants — a different purpose from NCL which covers the acute neonatal period.

(7) Annual leave (WTR 1998 Reg.13): Continues to accrue during NCL. The employer should not require the employee to use annual leave instead of NCL.

Employee-only right: Neonatal care leave applies to EMPLOYEES only, not to workers (limb b of ERA 1996 s.230(3)). However, the definition of 'employee' for statutory neonatal care pay purposes is wider — some individuals who do not qualify for leave may still qualify for pay if they meet the service and earnings conditions.
sections.keyPrinciple: Neonatal care leave adds up to 12 weeks on top of the existing statutory leave framework. A mother could potentially take: 52 weeks maternity leave + 12 weeks neonatal care leave = 64 weeks. A father/partner could take: 2 weeks paternity leave + 12 weeks neonatal care leave = 14 weeks. This is transformative for families of premature and sick babies.
refs: ERA 1996 Part 8 Chapter 5 (neonatal care leave)
ERA 1996 Part 8 Chapters 1-4 (maternity, paternity, adoption, shared parental leave)
Carer's Leave Act 2023 (separate right for long-term care needs)
ERA 1996 s.57A (time off for dependants — first 7 days bridge)
WTR 1998 Reg.13 (annual leave accrual)
Neonatal Care Leave Regulations 2025 Reg.13 (interaction provisions)
commonErrors: Requiring employees to use annual leave or unpaid parental leave instead of neonatal care leave — NCL is a separate and additional entitlement that cannot be substituted
Assuming neonatal care leave replaces maternity leave for the birth mother — it does NOT. It is additional. A mother takes maternity leave AND neonatal care leave

Not recognising the employee-only limitation — workers (limb b) are not eligible for neonatal care leave, though they may be eligible for neonatal care pay if they meet the wider 'employed earner' definition
Failing to plan for the operational impact of a combined 64-week absence (maternity + NCL) — employers should consider temporary cover arrangements
Overlooking the father/partner's entitlement — the non-birthing parent is equally entitled to neonatal care leave and should be actively informed of their rights

pendingAmendments.status: The Act came into force on 6 April 2025 and applies only to children born on or after that date. Given the nature of neonatal care leave (taken during or after the baby's hospitalisation), the first claims are unlikely to reach tribunal before late 2025 at the earliest. Key areas for judicial development: scope of 'neonatal care' (hospital vs home-based consultant care), interaction with maternity leave when both parents are on leave simultaneously, and application of enhanced redundancy protection.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — first decisions will establish interpretive baselines for this entirely new category of statutory leave

pendingAmendments.status: The ERA 2025 may affect the neonatal care leave framework through broader reforms to statutory leave entitlements, particularly Day 1 unfair dismissal rights (which would complement the existing Day 1 NCL entitlement) and Fair Work Agency enforcement powers.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — ERA 2025 reforms may strengthen the enforcement framework for NCL rights

pendingAmendments.status: The statutory neonatal care pay rate is reviewed annually alongside other statutory pay rates (SMP, SPP, SAP, ShPP). The 2025-26 rate is £187.18/week. The 2026-27 rate will be set by regulations in early 2026.
